1:
"What advantage then hath the Jew? or what profit is there of circumcision?" The witnesses note Paul's anticipated objection. Having shown (ch. 2) that the Jew, like the Gentile, is under sin and that circumcision profits nothing without obedience, the grammatical voices note Paul raises the natural objection: if so, what advantage has the Jew? What profit is circumcision?

On the question, the witnesses note the rhetorical method. Robertson and others note Paul, anticipating the Jewish objector, poses the question he will answer (v.2) — a diatribe style, raising and resolving objections.

The Reformed Calvin notes the tension: having seemingly stripped the Jews of all dignity, Paul now asks whether they had any advantage — and will answer that they did, but by God's free gift, not their own merit. The verse displays the anticipated objection: the advantage of the Jew.

The brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: having shown the Jew under sin and circumcision profitless without obedience (ch. 2), Paul raises the natural objection — what advantage has the Jew, what profit circumcision? — a rhetorical question he will answer (Robertson). Calvin notes the tension: having stripped the Jews of dignity, Paul asks whether they had any advantage, to answer it lay in God's free gift, not their merit. The verse displays the anticipated objection. The verse marks the diatribe method: raising the objection to resolve it. The verse displays the question: the Jew's advantage. The verse displays the tension: dignity stripped, yet advantage real. The anticipated objection displays Paul's careful method of meeting his readers' questions. The verse displays the apostle's fairness: granting the Jew's privileges. The verse displays the setup for the answer (v.2): the oracles of God. The verse displays the honest engagement: not evading the objection but facing it. The anticipated objection about the Jew's advantage displays Paul's careful, fair method of argument, raising the natural question that his teaching provoked, to answer that the Jew's true advantage lay in being entrusted with the oracles of God. The verse displays the apostle's readiness to grant the Jew his real privileges while maintaining that all are under sin. The verse sets up the answer (v.2) and the larger argument that the Jew's privileges did not exempt him from the universal need of justification by faith. The verse displays Paul's honest, methodical engagement with the Jewish objection.
2:
"Much every way: chiefly, because that unto them were committed the oracles of God." The witnesses note the Jew's chief advantage: the oracles of God. On "much every way," the grammatical voices note the affirmation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul affirms the Jew's advantage was much in every respect — and singles out the chief one.

On "the oracles of God," the witnesses note the supreme privilege. Barnes, Robertson, and Theodoret note "oracles" (logia) means the Scriptures of the Old Testament — the divine utterances, the law and the prophets, the promises of the Messiah, entrusted to the Jews. This was the highest honor: to be the depositories and dispensers of God's word.

The Reformed Calvin notes the value lay in the word, not the bare sign: the oracles were committed to them by God's free favour, not their merit; and our ingratitude is the worse if we receive God's word carelessly. The verse displays the Jew's chief advantage: the oracles of God.

The witnesses agree: the Jew's advantage was much in every way, chiefly that the oracles of God (logia, the Scriptures of the Old Testament — the law, prophets, and Messianic promises, the divine utterances) were committed to them, the supreme privilege of being the depositories and dispensers of God's word (Barnes, Robertson, Theodoret). Calvin notes the value lay in the word, not the bare sign, committed by God's free favour; our ingratitude is the worse if we receive his word carelessly. The verse displays the Jew's chief advantage. The verse marks the affirmation: much every way. The verse displays the supreme privilege: the oracles of God. The verse displays the honor: depositories of the word. The verse displays the source: God's free favour. The Jew's chief advantage — the oracles of God — displays the supreme privilege of possessing the Scriptures. The verse displays the trust: God confiding his word to Israel. The verse displays the responsibility: to keep and dispense the oracles. The verse displays the gratitude due: for the gift of the word. The Jew's chief advantage, the oracles of God committed to them, displays the supreme privilege of being entrusted with the Scriptures — the law, the prophets, and the Messianic promises — the highest honor a people could receive. The verse displays the value of God's word and the responsibility of those who possess it, and rebukes the carelessness of any who hold the Scriptures lightly. The verse sets up the further objection (v.3): does the Jews' unbelief void God's faithfulness?
3:
"For what if some did not believe? shall their unbelief make the faith of God without effect?" The witnesses note the objection from the Jews' unbelief. On "some did not believe," the grammatical voices note the limitation. Robertson and Theodoret note Paul says "some" — not all — were unbelieving (or unfaithful, apisteo can mean both); a softening that limits the failure to part of the nation.

On "the faith of God," the witnesses note the meaning. Robertson and Barnes note "the faith of God" here means God's faithfulness — does the Jews' unbelief nullify God's fidelity to his promises? The objection: if Israel fails, does God's covenant fail?

The Reformed Calvin notes the answer implied: God's truth is firm and constant, not destroyed by man's perfidy; though most broke the covenant, it retained its efficacy for the believing remnant. The verse displays the objection: does Israel's unbelief void God's faithfulness?

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul raises the objection — if some (not all, a softening, Robertson, Theodoret) Jews were unbelieving (or unfaithful), does their unbelief make void "the faith of God" (his faithfulness to his promises, Robertson, Barnes)? Calvin notes the answer: God's truth is firm, not destroyed by man's perfidy; the covenant retained its efficacy for the believing remnant. The verse displays the objection: does Israel's unbelief void God's faithfulness? The verse marks the limitation: some, not all. The verse displays the meaning: the faith of God his faithfulness. The verse displays the question: does man's failure nullify God's fidelity? The verse displays the implied answer: God remains faithful. The objection from Israel's unbelief displays the apostle's care to defend God's faithfulness. The verse displays the constancy of God: not voided by man's failure. The verse displays the remnant: the covenant efficacious for believers. The verse displays the principle: God's truth stands. The objection from the Jews' unbelief — does it make void the faithfulness of God? — displays Paul's defense of the divine fidelity: though some of Israel proved unbelieving, God's faithfulness to his promises stands firm and unshaken. The verse displays the great principle that man's failure cannot nullify God's truth, and sets up the emphatic vindication of God's faithfulness (v.4).
4:
"God forbid: yea, let God be true, but every man a liar; as it is written, That thou mightest be justified in thy sayings, and mightest overcome when thou art judged." The witnesses note the emphatic vindication of God's truth. On "God forbid," the grammatical voices note the strong denial. Robertson notes "me genoito" (may it not be) — Paul's vehement rejection of the idea that God's faithfulness could fail.

On "let God be true, but every man a liar," the witnesses note the principle. Gill and Barnes note the great affirmation: God is true and faithful, however false and inconstant all men may be — God's veracity stands though every man fail (echoing Psalm 116:11).

On the quotation from Psalm 51:4, the witnesses note the proof. Paul cites David's confession: God is justified in his sayings and overcomes when judged — God's righteousness vindicated, even man's sin serving to magnify it. The verse displays the emphatic vindication of God's truth against every man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul vehemently denies ("God forbid," me genoito, Robertson) that God's faithfulness could fail, affirming the great principle — let God be true though every man be a liar (God's veracity standing though all men fail, echoing Psalm 116:11, Gill, Barnes) — and proves it from Psalm 51:4: God is justified in his sayings and overcomes when judged, his righteousness vindicated. The verse displays the emphatic vindication of God's truth. The verse marks the strong denial: God forbid. The verse displays the principle: God true, every man a liar. The verse displays the proof: David's confession. The verse displays the vindication: God justified, overcoming. The emphatic vindication of God's truth displays the constancy of God against all human falsehood. The verse displays the contrast: God's truth, man's lie. The verse displays the scriptural ground: Psalm 51. The verse displays the magnifying of God: even sin serving his glory. The emphatic vindication of God's truth — let God be true, but every man a liar — displays the unshakable faithfulness and righteousness of God against all human falsehood and failure. The verse displays the great principle that God's veracity stands though every man prove false, and that God is justified and overcomes even when men presume to judge him. The verse sets up the objection that this magnifying of God by man's sin might excuse the sinner (vv.5-8).
5:
"But if our unrighteousness commend the righteousness of God, what shall we say? Is God unrighteous who taketh vengeance? (I speak as a man.)" The witnesses note the objection: if sin magnifies God, is God unjust to punish? The grammatical voices note the perverse inference. Barnes and Robertson note the objector argues: if our unrighteousness sets off (commends) God's righteousness by contrast, is God then unjust to inflict wrath? — a sophistical attempt to escape judgment.

On "I speak as a man," the witnesses note Paul's qualification. Paul adds this to mark the objection as a human, carnal way of reasoning — not his own view, but the cavil he must answer; he speaks after the manner of men, putting the objection he abhors.

The Reformed Calvin notes the danger: the perverse reasoning that would make God's glory an excuse for sin; Paul rejects it as blasphemous. The verse displays the objection: does sin's magnifying of God excuse the sinner?

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the objector argues that if our unrighteousness commends (sets off by contrast) God's righteousness, then God is unjust to take vengeance (Barnes, Robertson) — a sophistical attempt to escape judgment; Paul adds "I speak as a man," marking it a human, carnal cavil, not his own view but the objection he must answer. Calvin notes the danger: the perverse reasoning making God's glory an excuse for sin, which Paul rejects as blasphemous. The verse displays the objection. The verse marks the perverse inference: sin magnifying God excuses the sinner. The verse displays the qualification: I speak as a man. The verse displays the sophistry: escaping judgment by abusing God's glory. The objection displays the perversity of reasoning that abuses God's glory to excuse sin. The verse displays Paul's care: marking the cavil as human. The verse displays the danger: the antinomian misuse of grace. The verse displays the apostle's method: facing the objection to refute it. The objection — that if sin magnifies God's righteousness, God is unjust to punish — displays the perverse, sophistical reasoning that would abuse God's glory to excuse the sinner. The verse displays Paul's care to distinguish this carnal cavil from his own teaching, and sets up his emphatic rejection of it (v.6). The verse displays the apostle facing the hardest objections to his doctrine of grace.
6:
"God forbid: for then how shall God judge the world?" The brief verse notes Paul's emphatic rejection of the objection. On "God forbid," the grammatical voices note the strong denial. Robertson notes the vehement rejection (me genoito) of the idea that God could be unjust.

On "how shall God judge the world?" the witnesses note the argument. Barnes, Calvin, and JFB note the reductio: if God were unjust to punish sin that magnifies his glory, he could not judge the world at all — yet it is axiomatic (even to the Jew) that God will judge the world. The objection therefore destroys itself.

The witnesses note the principle: God's right to judge is certain; therefore he cannot be unjust in punishing sin. The verse displays the rejection of the objection by appeal to God's judgment of the world.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul vehemently rejects the objection ("God forbid," me genoito, Robertson) by a reductio — if God were unjust to punish sin, he could not judge the world at all; yet it is axiomatic that God will judge the world (Barnes, Calvin, JFB). The objection therefore destroys itself. The verse displays the rejection of the objection. The verse marks the strong denial: God forbid. The verse displays the argument: God's judgment of the world is certain. The verse displays the reductio: the objection would make judgment impossible. The verse displays the axiom: God will judge. The rejection of the objection displays the certainty of God's righteous judgment. The verse displays the principle: God cannot be unjust. The verse displays the appeal: to the universal expectation of judgment. The verse displays the self-destruction of the cavil. The rejection of the objection by appeal to God's judgment of the world displays the certainty of God's righteousness: since God will surely judge the world (a truth no one denies), he cannot be unjust in punishing the sin that, against the sinner's will, magnifies his glory. The verse displays the apostle's swift refutation of the antinomian cavil, grounded in the universal certainty of divine judgment. The verse continues the dialogue with the objector (vv.7-8).
7:
"For if the truth of God hath more abounded through my lie unto his glory; why yet am I also judged as a sinner?" The witnesses note the objection restated and sharpened. The grammatical voices note the repeated cavil. Barnes and Robertson note the objector presses: if my falsehood (sin) makes God's truth abound more to his glory, why am I still condemned as a sinner? — the same perverse reasoning, now in the first person.

On the sophistry, the witnesses note the perversity. The argument would make sin a benefit (since it magnifies God) and condemnation unjust — a plausible-sounding cavil that Paul exposes.

The witnesses note Paul's purpose: to bring the objection to its absurd conclusion (v.8), showing where such reasoning leads. The verse displays the objection sharpened: why am I judged if my sin glorifies God?

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the objector presses the cavil in the first person — if my falsehood (sin) makes God's truth abound more to his glory, why am I still judged as a sinner? (Barnes, Robertson) — the same perverse reasoning that would make sin a benefit and condemnation unjust. Paul restates it to bring it to its absurd conclusion (v.8). The verse displays the objection sharpened. The verse marks the repeated cavil: sin magnifying God's glory. The verse displays the sophistry: condemnation made unjust. The verse displays the perversity: sin made a benefit. The verse displays Paul's purpose: exposing the reasoning. The sharpened objection displays the perversity of using God's glory to excuse sin. The verse displays the plausible cavil: my sin serves God's truth. The verse displays the false conclusion: therefore I should not be judged. The verse displays the setup for refutation (v.8). The objection sharpened — why am I judged if my sin makes God's truth abound to his glory? — displays the perverse sophistry that would turn the magnifying of God into an excuse for the sinner. The verse displays Paul's method of pressing the cavil to its absurd and blasphemous conclusion, that its falsity may be plain. The verse leads to the slanderous extreme (v.8): let us do evil that good may come.
8:
"And not rather, (as we be slanderously reported, and as some affirm that we say,) Let us do evil, that good may come? whose damnation is just." The witnesses note the slanderous charge and Paul's stern verdict. On "as we be slanderously reported," the grammatical voices note the calumny. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's opponents slanderously accused him of teaching "let us do evil that good may come" — the antinomian distortion of his doctrine of grace, which he indignantly repudiates.

On "let us do evil, that good may come," the witnesses note the perverse maxim. This is the absurd conclusion the cavil leads to: if sin magnifies God, then let us sin freely. Paul exposes it as the blasphemous distortion it is.

On "whose damnation is just," the witnesses note the stern verdict. Calvin and Robertson note Paul pronounces the condemnation of those who hold or charge such a doctrine just — a curt, severe dismissal of the slander. The verse displays the slanderous charge and Paul's just condemnation of it.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's opponents slanderously reported that he taught "let us do evil that good may come" — the antinomian distortion of his doctrine of grace (Barnes, Robertson) — the absurd conclusion of the cavil (if sin magnifies God, let us sin freely); and Paul pronounces "whose damnation is just," a stern, curt verdict on those who hold or charge such doctrine (Calvin, Robertson). The verse displays the slanderous charge and Paul's just condemnation. The verse marks the calumny: the antinomian distortion. The verse displays the perverse maxim: do evil that good may come. The verse displays the verdict: their damnation just. The verse displays the repudiation: indignant rejection of the slander. The slanderous charge and Paul's verdict display his abhorrence of antinomianism. The verse displays the distortion: grace twisted into license. The verse displays the severity: just condemnation. The verse displays the apostle's indignation: repudiating the calumny. The slanderous charge — that Paul taught "let us do evil that good may come" — and his stern verdict that such teaching's damnation is just, display his utter abhorrence of antinomianism, the distortion of grace into license. The verse displays the apostle's indignant repudiation of the calumny and his severe condemnation of those who would turn the gospel of grace into an excuse for sin. The verse closes the dialogue and turns to the proof that all, Jew and Gentile, are under sin (vv.9-20).
9:
"What then? are we better than they? No, in no wise: for we have before proved both Jews and Gentiles, that they are all under sin;" The witnesses note the conclusion: all are under sin. On "are we better than they?" the grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks whether the Jews have any preference over the Gentiles regarding justification — and answers, "No, in no wise" (ou pantos, not at all).

On "we have before proved," the witnesses note the prior argument. Robertson notes the verb (proetiasametha, to charge beforehand) — Paul had already charged both Jews (ch. 2) and Gentiles (ch. 1) with being under sin, the conclusion of his whole argument.

On "all under sin," the eastern Chrysostom and Theodoret note the universal verdict: all, Jew and Gentile, are under sin — under its guilt, power, and condemnation, justly arraigned before God's tribunal. The verse displays the conclusion: all under sin.

The witnesses agree: Paul asks whether the Jews are better than the Gentiles regarding justification, and answers "No, in no wise" (ou pantos, not at all) — for he has already charged (proetiasametha, charged beforehand, Robertson) both Jews (ch. 2) and Gentiles (ch. 1) with being all under sin, under its guilt, power, and condemnation (Chrysostom, Theodoret). The verse displays the conclusion: all under sin. The verse marks the question: are the Jews better? The verse displays the answer: not at all. The verse displays the prior proof: both charged with sin. The verse displays the universal verdict: all under sin. The conclusion that all are under sin displays the leveling of Jew and Gentile before God. The verse displays the charge: both under sin's dominion. The verse displays the equality: no preference in guilt. The verse displays the foundation: all need justification. The conclusion that all, Jew and Gentile, are under sin displays the great leveling truth of Paul's argument: none is better, all are under the guilt, power, and condemnation of sin, justly arraigned before God. The verse displays the universal verdict that prepares for the universal remedy — justification by faith for all. The verse sets up the scriptural proof of universal sin (vv.10-18).
10:
"As it is written, There is none righteous, no, not one:" The witnesses note the opening of the scriptural catena on universal sin. On "as it is written," the grammatical voices note the proof from Scripture. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, to prove all are under sin, strings together a series of Old Testament quotations (a catena, chiefly from the Psalms and Isaiah) — beginning with Psalm 14:1-3.

On "none righteous, no, not one," the witnesses note the comprehensive verdict. The emphatic statement leaves no exception: not one person is righteous before God. Calvin notes this establishes that all are destitute of righteousness.

The witnesses note Paul's method: proving from the Jews' own Scriptures (which they revered) that all are sinners — so the conclusion cannot be denied. The verse displays the opening verdict: none righteous, not one.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, to prove all are under sin, opens a catena of Old Testament quotations (chiefly Psalms and Isaiah, beginning Psalm 14, Barnes, Robertson) with the comprehensive verdict — "none righteous, no, not one" — leaving no exception; proving from the Jews' own revered Scriptures that all are sinners (Calvin). The verse displays the opening verdict. The verse marks the method: proof from Scripture. The verse displays the catena: a chain of quotations. The verse displays the verdict: none righteous. The verse displays the emphasis: no, not one. The opening verdict displays the universal sinfulness proved from Scripture. The verse displays the comprehensiveness: not one exception. The verse displays the authority: the Jews' own Scriptures. The verse displays the foundation: all destitute of righteousness. The opening verdict of the catena — none righteous, no, not one — displays the universal sinfulness of mankind, proved from the Jews' own revered Scriptures so that none can deny it. The verse displays the emphatic, exceptionless declaration that not one person is righteous before God, the foundation of Paul's argument that justification cannot come by works but only by grace through faith. The verse begins the chain of scriptural witnesses to universal sin (vv.10-18). Paul lets the Scriptures themselves pronounce the sentence, that the verdict might fall with the weight of God own word and silence every objector.
11:
"There is none that understandeth, there is none that seeketh after God." The brief verse continues the catena on universal sin. The grammatical voices note the two-fold deficiency. Barnes and Robertson note the quotation (Psalm 14:2) declares none understands (lacks spiritual discernment) and none seeks after God — the mind darkened, the will averse.

On "none that understandeth," the witnesses note the corruption of the mind. Apart from grace, none truly understands divine things — the natural man is blind to God.

On "none that seeketh after God," the witnesses note the corruption of the will. Calvin and others note none, by nature, seeks God; man flees from him rather than seeking him. The verse displays the universal failure of understanding and seeking.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catena continues (Psalm 14:2) — none understands (lacks spiritual discernment, the mind darkened) and none seeks after God (the will averse, fleeing rather than seeking, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). The verse displays the universal failure of understanding and seeking. The verse marks the two-fold deficiency: mind and will. The verse displays the darkened mind: none understands. The verse displays the averse will: none seeks God. The verse displays the natural state: blind and unwilling. The universal failure of understanding and seeking displays the corruption of both mind and will. The verse displays the blindness: none discerns divine things. The verse displays the aversion: none seeks God by nature. The verse displays the need of grace: to enlighten and draw. The universal failure of understanding and seeking after God displays the corruption of human nature in both mind and will — the understanding darkened so that none discerns divine things, the will averse so that none, by nature, seeks God. The verse displays the depth of the fall and the necessity of divine grace to enlighten the blind and draw the unwilling. The verse continues the catena's portrait of universal depravity (vv.12-18). The catena thus traces the ruin to its seat in the inmost faculties, that no man might imagine some sound remnant in himself by which to be justified before God.
12:
"They are all gone out of the way, they are together become unprofitable; there is none that doeth good, no, not one." The witnesses note the continuation of the catena: universal corruption. The grammatical voices note the comprehensive declaration. Barnes and Robertson note the quotation (Psalm 14:3) — all have gone astray, all become unprofitable (useless, corrupt), none does good, not one.

On "gone out of the way," the witnesses note the apostasy. All have turned aside from the right way, wandering from God like lost sheep.

On "none that doeth good, no, not one," the eastern Cyril and Theodoret note the universal verdict repeated: not one does good — the emphatic exclusion of any exception, the corruption total. The verse displays the universal corruption: all astray, none doing good.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catena continues (Psalm 14:3) — all have gone out of the way (apostasy, wandering from God), all become unprofitable (useless, corrupt), none does good, no, not one (Barnes, Robertson; Cyril, Theodoret noting the emphatic exclusion of any exception). The verse displays the universal corruption. The verse marks the apostasy: all gone astray. The verse displays the uselessness: all become unprofitable. The verse displays the verdict: none does good. The verse displays the emphasis: not one. The universal corruption displays the total depravity of mankind apart from grace. The verse displays the wandering: all turned aside. The verse displays the corruption: all unprofitable. The verse displays the exceptionless verdict: not one good. The universal corruption of mankind — all gone astray, all become unprofitable, none doing good, not one — displays the total depravity of human nature apart from grace, every man turned from the right way and become corrupt. The verse displays the emphatic, exceptionless declaration that not one does good, completing the portrait of universal sinfulness that leaves all without excuse and in need of the righteousness of God. The verse continues the catena (vv.13-18). By piling quotation upon quotation Paul forecloses every escape, leaving the whole race shut up together under the one indictment of universal guilt before its Maker.
13:
"Their throat is an open sepulchre; with their tongues they have used deceit; the poison of asps is under their lips:" The witnesses note the catena's portrait of corrupt speech. The grammatical voices note the vivid imagery. Barnes and Robertson note the quotations (Psalm 5:9; 140:3) describing the sins of the tongue — the throat an open sepulchre (exhaling corruption and death), the tongue used for deceit, the poison of asps under the lips.

On "open sepulchre," the witnesses note the corruption. The throat like an open grave, breathing out death and putrefaction — corrupt, deadly speech.

On "poison of asps," the eastern Theodoret and others note the venom. The lips conceal deadly poison, like the asp — slanderous, malicious, destructive words. The verse displays the corruption of human speech.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catena describes corrupt speech (Psalm 5:9; 140:3) — the throat an open sepulchre (exhaling corruption and death), the tongue used for deceit, the poison of asps under the lips (deadly, slanderous words, Barnes, Robertson, Theodoret). The verse displays the corruption of human speech. The verse marks the imagery: throat, tongue, lips. The verse displays the open sepulchre: deadly speech. The verse displays the deceit: the tongue's falsehood. The verse displays the poison: venomous words. The corruption of human speech displays the sin that flows from the depraved heart through the mouth. The verse displays the death: speech like an open grave. The verse displays the falsehood: deceitful tongues. The verse displays the venom: poisonous lips. The corruption of human speech — the throat an open sepulchre, the tongue deceitful, the lips full of poison — displays the sin that pours from the depraved heart through the organs of speech, corrupt, deceitful, and deadly. The verse displays the vivid scriptural portrait of the tongue's wickedness, a further proof of the universal depravity that makes all guilty before God. The verse continues the catena's catalogue of sin (vv.14-18). Each member named — throat, tongue, lips — becomes a separate witness, that the sin charged might be seen to pervade the whole man and not some single faculty alone.
14:
"Whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness:" The brief verse continues the catena's portrait of corrupt speech. The grammatical voices note the quotation. Barnes and Ellicott note the citation (Psalm 10:7) — the mouth full of cursing and bitterness, the speech of the wicked overflowing with imprecation and rancor.

On "cursing and bitterness," the witnesses note the malice. The mouth pours forth cursing (imprecations against others and God) and bitterness (rancor, malice) — the overflow of a corrupt heart.

The witnesses note the continuation of the indictment: the sins of speech testifying to the universal corruption. The verse displays the mouth full of cursing and bitterness.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the catena continues (Psalm 10:7) — the mouth full of cursing and bitterness, the speech of the wicked overflowing with imprecation and rancor, the overflow of a corrupt heart (Barnes, Ellicott). The verse displays the mouth full of cursing and bitterness. The verse marks the citation: Psalm 10. The verse displays the cursing: imprecation against God and men. The verse displays the bitterness: rancor and malice. The verse displays the overflow: from a corrupt heart. The mouth full of cursing and bitterness displays the malice that pours from the depraved heart. The verse displays the imprecation: cursing speech. The verse displays the rancor: bitter words. The verse displays the source: the corrupt heart. The mouth full of cursing and bitterness displays the malice and rancor that overflow from the corrupt human heart through the speech — a further witness in the catena to the universal sinfulness of mankind. The verse displays the wickedness of the tongue, pouring forth imprecation and bitterness, and continues the scriptural proof that all are under sin and guilty before God. The verse leads to the catena's portrait of violent deeds (vv.15-17). The catalogue of the sins of speech and action together displays the thoroughness of human corruption that Paul proves from the Scriptures. Thus the catalogue moves inward to outward, from the corrupt fountain of the heart to the bitter streams of the mouth, sealing the proof of a depravity that spares no part of human nature.
15:
"Their feet are swift to shed blood:" The brief verse turns the catena to violent deeds. The grammatical voices note the quotation. Barnes and Robertson note the citation (Isaiah 59:7; Proverbs 1:16) — the feet swift to shed blood, the readiness and eagerness of the wicked to commit violence and murder.

On "swift to shed blood," the witnesses note the eagerness. The feet hasten to violence — not reluctant but eager, running to do harm and shed blood.

The witnesses note the progression: from corrupt speech (vv.13-14) to violent action (v.15) — the catena tracing sin from heart to mouth to deed. The verse displays the feet swift to shed blood.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the catena turns to violent deeds (Isaiah 59:7; Proverbs 1:16) — the feet swift to shed blood, the eager readiness of the wicked to commit violence and murder, hastening to harm (Barnes, Robertson). The verse displays the feet swift to shed blood. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah 59. The verse displays the violence: shedding blood. The verse displays the eagerness: swift, hastening. The verse displays the progression: from speech to deed. The feet swift to shed blood display the violence that flows from the corrupt heart into action. The verse displays the readiness: eager for harm. The verse displays the haste: running to violence. The verse displays the deeds: bloodshed and murder. The feet swift to shed blood display the violent deeds that flow from the corrupt human heart — not reluctant but eager, hastening to harm and bloodshed. The verse displays the progression of the catena from corrupt speech to violent action, tracing sin from the heart through the mouth to the deeds, a further proof of the universal depravity that makes all guilty before God. The verse continues the portrait of human corruption (vv.16-18). The swift feet complete the dreadful portrait of a nature wholly bent toward evil, eager in the very pursuit of the harm that Scripture everywhere lays to the charge of fallen man.
16:
"Destruction and misery are in their ways:" The brief verse continues the catena's portrait of sin's ruin. The grammatical voices note the quotation. Barnes and Calvin note the citation (Isaiah 59:7) — destruction and misery mark the paths of the wicked, the wreckage they leave behind.

On "destruction and misery," the witnesses note the fruit of sin. Wherever the wicked go, they leave destruction (ruin to others) and misery (wretchedness) — sin's path strewn with wreckage.

The witnesses note the testimony: sin produces not blessing but ruin, both to others and to the sinner. The verse displays destruction and misery in the ways of the wicked.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the catena continues (Isaiah 59:7) — destruction and misery in their ways, the wreckage the wicked leave behind, sin's path strewn with ruin and wretchedness (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays destruction and misery in the ways of the wicked. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah 59. The verse displays the destruction: ruin to others. The verse displays the misery: wretchedness. The verse displays the path: strewn with wreckage. Destruction and misery in their ways display the ruinous fruit of sin. The verse displays the wreckage: left by the wicked. The verse displays the wretchedness: sin's misery. The verse displays the testimony: sin produces ruin. Destruction and misery in the ways of the wicked display the ruinous fruit of sin — wherever the corrupt go, they leave destruction to others and misery in their wake. The verse displays the testimony of Scripture that sin produces not blessing but wreckage, both to the sinner and to those around, a further proof of the depravity that makes all guilty before God. The verse continues the catena toward its climax (vv.17-18). The portrait of destruction marks the bitter harvest of the universal sin Paul proves. So the path of the wicked is marked at every step by the wreckage they leave, a standing witness that sin is by its nature ruinous, working death wherever it is suffered to reign. The verse stands as one more stone in the unanswerable case Paul builds against all flesh.
17:
"And the way of peace have they not known:" The brief verse continues the catena's portrait of sin. The grammatical voices note the quotation. Barnes and Calvin note the citation (Isaiah 59:8) — the way of peace they have not known, the wicked strangers to true peace, both with God and among men.

On "the way of peace," the witnesses note the deprivation. The wicked, given to violence and destruction (vv.15-16), do not know the way of peace — neither peace with God nor peace with their fellow men.

The witnesses note the contrast: sin produces strife and ruin, not peace; the path of the wicked is the opposite of the way of peace. The verse displays the wicked as strangers to the way of peace.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the catena continues (Isaiah 59:8) — the way of peace they have not known, the wicked strangers to true peace, both with God and among men, given to violence rather than peace (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays the wicked as strangers to the way of peace. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah 59. The verse displays the deprivation: peace unknown. The verse displays the contrast: violence, not peace. The verse displays the scope: peace with God and men. The wicked as strangers to peace display the fruitlessness of sin. The verse displays the ignorance: the way of peace unknown. The verse displays the strife: the opposite of peace. The verse displays the alienation: from God and men. The wicked as strangers to the way of peace display the fruitlessness and alienation of sin — those given to violence and destruction know nothing of true peace, neither peace with God nor peace among men. The verse displays the contrast between the path of the wicked and the way of peace, a further witness in the catena to the universal corruption of mankind. The verse leads to the catena's climax: no fear of God (v.18). The portrait of the peaceless wicked completes the scriptural proof of universal sin.
18:
"There is no fear of God before their eyes." The witnesses note the climax of the catena: no fear of God. On "no fear of God," the grammatical voices note the root. Barnes, Robertson, and JFB note the citation (Psalm 36:1) — the root and summary of all the foregoing sins: no fear of God before their eyes. The absence of the fear of God is the source of all the corruption described.

On the position, the witnesses note this is the climax. Calvin and others note the catena culminates here: the want of the fear of God is the fountain from which all the sins of mind, speech, and deed flow.

The puritan Henry notes: where no fear of God is, no good is to be looked for. The fear of God is the beginning of wisdom; its absence is the root of all evil. The verse displays the climax of the catena: no fear of God before their eyes.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catena climaxes (Psalm 36:1) with the root and summary of all the foregoing sins — no fear of God before their eyes; the absence of the fear of God the fountain from which all corruption of mind, speech, and deed flows (Barnes, Robertson, JFB, Calvin). Henry notes: where no fear of God is, no good is to be looked for. The verse displays the climax: no fear of God. The verse marks the root: the source of all sin. The verse displays the summary: the catena's culmination. The verse displays the fountain: all corruption flowing from this. The climax of the catena — no fear of God — displays the root of all human sin. The verse displays the absence: the fear of God gone. The verse displays the consequence: all corruption follows. The verse displays the principle: the fear of God the beginning of wisdom. The climax of the catena — there is no fear of God before their eyes — displays the root and fountain of all human sin: the absence of the fear of God, from which flow all the corruptions of mind, speech, and deed described in the foregoing verses. The verse displays the great principle that where no fear of God is, no good can be looked for, completing the scriptural proof that all are under sin. The verse closes the catena and sets up the conclusion: the law stops every mouth (v.19).
19:
"Now we know that what things soever the law saith, it saith to them who are under the law: that every mouth may be stopped, and all the world may become guilty before God." The witnesses note the purpose of the law: to stop every mouth. On "the law saith to them under the law," the grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note the law speaks to those under it (the Jews especially) — so the Jew cannot evade the indictment by claiming the quoted Scriptures applied only to Gentiles.

On "that every mouth may be stopped," the witnesses note the first purpose. The law's effect is to silence all excuse and self-justification — every mouth stopped, no plea remaining.

On "all the world may become guilty before God," the witnesses note the universal verdict. The eastern Chrysostom and the puritan Henry note the law brings the whole world (Jew and Gentile) under guilt and condemnation before God — all arraigned, all silenced, all guilty. The verse displays the law's purpose: stopping every mouth, all the world guilty.

The witnesses agree: the law speaks to those under it (the Jews especially, Barnes, Robertson) — so the Jew cannot evade the indictment — that every mouth may be stopped (all excuse and self-justification silenced) and all the world become guilty before God (the whole world, Jew and Gentile, brought under condemnation, Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays the law's purpose: stopping every mouth. The verse marks the application: the law to those under it. The verse displays the first effect: every mouth stopped. The verse displays the verdict: all the world guilty. The verse displays the universality: Jew and Gentile alike. The law's purpose displays its function: to silence all and convict all. The verse displays the silencing: no plea remaining. The verse displays the guilt: all the world arraigned. The verse displays the leveling: none exempt. The law's purpose — to stop every mouth and bring all the world guilty before God — displays the convicting function of the law: silencing all excuse and self-justification, arraigning the whole world (Jew and Gentile) under guilt and condemnation. The verse displays the great conclusion of Paul's argument that all are under sin, preparing for the declaration that no flesh can be justified by the law (v.20) and the way of justification by faith (v.21ff). The verse displays the law shutting up all under sin that grace might be sought.
20:
"Therefore by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified in his sight: for by the law is the knowledge of sin." The witnesses note the great negative conclusion: no justification by law. On "by the deeds of the law no flesh be justified," the grammatical voices note the universal exclusion. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note no one (no flesh, ou pasa sarx) can be justified before God by works of law — Jew or Gentile, by any law. This refers to the moral law, not merely ceremonies.

On "the deeds of the law" — moral or ceremonial? — the witnesses debate. Calvin argues strongly (against Chrysostom, Origen, Jerome) that Paul means the whole law, moral included, not just ceremonies — for the sins charged were against the moral law. Theodoret notes both moral and ceremonial precepts, but the ceremonial cannot justify.

On "by the law is the knowledge of sin," the witnesses note the law's true function. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note the law's office is not to justify but to reveal sin — to show men their guilt, like a mirror or straight-edge exposing crookedness. The verse displays the great conclusion: no justification by law, which gives only the knowledge of sin.

The witnesses agree: no flesh (no one, Jew or Gentile) can be justified before God by the deeds of the law, for the law's office is to give the knowledge of sin, not justification (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott). The grammatical-historical voices and Calvin take "deeds of the law" as the whole law (moral included), not merely ceremonies — Calvin arguing strongly against Chrysostom and Origen that the sins charged were moral; Theodoret notes both moral and ceremonial precepts, the ceremonial unable to justify. The verse displays the great negative conclusion. The verse marks the exclusion: no flesh justified by law. The verse displays the debate: moral vs ceremonial law. The verse displays the law's function: the knowledge of sin. The verse displays the mirror: law exposing guilt. The great conclusion displays the impossibility of justification by works and the true purpose of the law. The verse displays the universal exclusion: none justified by law. The verse displays the law's office: revealing, not removing, sin. The verse displays the turn: from law's condemnation to grace. The great negative conclusion — no flesh justified by the deeds of the law, which gives only the knowledge of sin — displays the impossibility of justification by works and the true convicting function of the law: to expose sin and shut men up to grace. The verse displays the hinge of Paul's argument, turning from the universal condemnation under law to the way of justification by faith apart from law (v.21ff). The verse displays the law's purpose to reveal sin and drive sinners to Christ.
21:
"But now the righteousness of God without the law is manifested, being witnessed by the law and the prophets;" The witnesses note the great turn: the righteousness of God apart from law. On "but now," the grammatical voices note the transition. Robertson and Ellicott note the emphatic turn (nun de) — having proved all under sin and no justification by law, Paul now sets forth God's way of justification, the gospel age.

On "the righteousness of God without the law," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Calvin note this is God's plan of justifying men, apart from works of law — the same "righteousness of God" of 1:17, now fully unfolded. It is "without the law" — not by personal obedience, but by another way.

On "witnessed by the law and the prophets," the witnesses note the continuity. The eastern Theodoret and the puritan Henry note this righteousness, though apart from law, was foretold and attested by the law and the prophets (Habakkuk, the sacrifices, the promises) — no novelty, but the Old Testament's own witness. The verse displays the righteousness of God manifested apart from law.

The witnesses agree: having proved all under sin, Paul now (the emphatic "but now," nun de, Robertson, Ellicott) sets forth the righteousness of God apart from law — God's plan of justifying men, not by personal obedience but by another way (the righteousness of 1:17 unfolded, Barnes, Calvin) — yet witnessed by the law and the prophets (foretold and attested in the Old Testament, no novelty, Theodoret, Henry). The verse displays the righteousness of God manifested apart from law. The verse marks the turn: from condemnation to grace. The verse displays the meaning: God's plan of justification. The verse displays the independence: without the law. The verse displays the continuity: witnessed by law and prophets. The righteousness of God apart from law displays the way of justification and its scriptural pedigree. The verse displays the manifestation: now made plain in the gospel. The verse displays the apartness: not by works. The verse displays the witness: the Old Testament's testimony. The righteousness of God manifested apart from law, yet witnessed by the law and the prophets, displays the way of justification at the heart of the gospel: God's own righteousness, freely given, received not by works but by another way, foretold in the Old Testament and now fully revealed. The verse displays the great turn from the universal condemnation under law to the gracious provision of God, and sets up the full statement of justification by faith (vv.22-26).
22:
"Even the righteousness of God which is by faith of Jesus Christ unto all and upon all them that believe: for there is no difference:" The witnesses note the righteousness by faith, for all who believe. On "by faith of Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the objective genitive. Robertson, Barnes, and Calvin note "faith of Jesus Christ" means faith in Jesus Christ (the objective genitive, as "faith of God" = faith in God) — not Christ's own faith, but faith directed to him as its object; faith the instrument, not the merit.

On "unto all and upon all that believe," the witnesses note the universality. Barnes and Theodoret note the righteousness comes unto all (offered to all, Jew and Gentile) and upon all that believe (applied to all believers) — some take "unto all" of Jews, "upon all" of Gentiles (Theodoret), but the point is its universal reach to all who believe.

On "no difference," the witnesses note the leveling. There is no distinction between Jew and Gentile regarding justification: all must be justified the same way, by faith. The verse displays the righteousness by faith, for all who believe without difference.

The witnesses agree: the righteousness of God is by faith in Jesus Christ (the objective genitive — faith directed to Christ as its object, not Christ's own faith, faith the instrument not the merit, Robertson, Barnes, Calvin) unto all and upon all that believe (offered and applied to all believers, Jew and Gentile; Theodoret taking "unto all" of Jews, "upon all" of Gentiles), for there is no difference — no distinction regarding justification, all justified the same way. The verse displays the righteousness by faith for all who believe. The verse marks the instrument: faith in Christ. The verse displays the object: Jesus Christ. The verse displays the universality: unto and upon all who believe. The verse displays the leveling: no difference. The righteousness by faith for all displays the instrument and universal reach of justification. The verse displays the objective genitive: faith in Christ. The verse displays the application: to all believers. The verse displays the equality: Jew and Gentile alike. The righteousness of God by faith in Jesus Christ, unto all and upon all that believe without difference, displays the instrument of justification (faith directed to Christ) and its universal reach to all believers, Jew and Gentile alike. The verse displays the great leveling truth that there is no distinction in the way of salvation: all are justified the same way, by faith in Christ. The verse sets up the reason for the leveling: all have sinned (v.23).
23:
"For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God;" The witnesses note the universal ground of the leveling: all have sinned. On "all have sinned," the grammatical voices note the comprehensive verdict. Robertson and Gill note the aorist (hemarton) gathers the whole race into one statement — all sinned (in Adam and in their own persons), the universal fact that levels Jew and Gentile.

On "come short of the glory of God," the witnesses debate the meaning. Barnes, Calvin, and Robertson take "the glory of God" as the praise or approbation of God (John 12:43) — all fall short of God's approval; others take it as the glory God bestows, or the honor due to God. The present tense (husterountai) marks the continuing deficiency.

The witnesses note the foundation: since all have sinned, all are equally dependent on grace, and none can be justified by works. The verse displays the universal ground: all have sinned and fall short.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: all have sinned (the aorist gathering the whole race, in Adam and in their own persons, Robertson, Gill) and come short of the glory of God — most taking "glory of God" as his praise or approbation (John 12:43, Barnes, Calvin, Robertson), all falling short of God's approval, the present tense marking the continuing deficiency. The verse displays the universal ground: all have sinned. The verse marks the verdict: all sinned. The verse displays the deficiency: come short. The verse displays the debate: God's approval, or the glory he gives. The verse displays the foundation: all dependent on grace. The universal ground displays the reason none can be justified by works. The verse displays the comprehensiveness: all sinned. The verse displays the shortfall: of God's glory. The verse displays the leveling: all equally guilty. The universal ground of the leveling — all have sinned and come short of the glory of God — displays the reason there is no difference in the way of justification: since every man is a sinner falling short of God's approval, all are equally dependent on grace and none can be justified by works. The verse displays the great foundation of the gospel: the universal sinfulness that makes the universal remedy of justification by faith necessary. The verse sets up the gracious provision: justified freely (v.24).
24:
"Being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus:" The witnesses note the gracious provision: justified freely through redemption. On "justified freely by his grace," the grammatical voices note the gratuitous gift. Barnes, Robertson, and Wesley note "freely" (dorean) means as a free, undeserved gift, not earned or merited — justification flowing from God's grace (his unmerited favour), the moving cause.

On "the redemption that is in Christ Jesus," the witnesses note the means. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note "redemption" (apolutrosis) means a releasing by ransom — the price paid to set captives free. The deliverance from sin's bondage and guilt is effected by the ransom Christ paid (his blood, v.25).

The Reformed Calvin notes the causes: God's mercy the efficient cause, Christ's blood the meritorious, faith the instrumental, God's glory the final. Theodoret: Christ offered his body as the price of redemption. The verse displays the gracious provision: justified freely through Christ's redemption.

The witnesses agree: believers are justified freely (dorean, as a free undeserved gift, not earned, Barnes, Robertson, Wesley) by God's grace (his unmerited favour, the moving cause) through the redemption (apolutrosis, a releasing by ransom, the price paid to free captives, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) that is in Christ Jesus. Calvin notes the causes: God's mercy efficient, Christ's blood meritorious, faith instrumental, God's glory final. Theodoret: Christ offered his body as the price of redemption. The verse displays the gracious provision. The verse marks the gift: justified freely. The verse displays the source: God's grace. The verse displays the means: the redemption in Christ. The verse displays the ransom: the price paid. The gracious provision displays justification as a free gift through Christ's ransom. The verse displays the gratuity: without merit. The verse displays the grace: God's free favour. The verse displays the redemption: deliverance by ransom. Justification freely by grace through the redemption in Christ Jesus displays the heart of the gospel: sinners, unable to justify themselves, are justified as a free gift, flowing from God's unmerited grace and effected by the ransom Christ paid to deliver them from sin's bondage and guilt. The verse displays the perfect harmony of grace (the source) and redemption (the means), and sets up the fullest statement of the atonement: the propitiation through faith in Christ's blood (v.25).
25:
"Whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation through faith in his blood, to declare his righteousness for the remission of sins that are past, through the forbearance of God;" The witnesses note the great atonement passage — and its central terms. On "propitiation" (hilasterion), the grammatical voices debate. Barnes, Clarke, and Theodoret take it as the mercy-seat (the lid of the ark, where atonement was made, where God was reconciled) — Christ the true mercy-seat. Robertson and Calvin lean to "propitiatory sacrifice" or "propitiatory gift" — that which renders God propitious, the offering by which God is reconciled. Both agree on the substance: Christ the means of propitiation.

On "set forth" (proetheto), the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Robertson take it as "set forth publicly" (exhibited openly on the cross); Calvin allows also "purposed beforehand" (foreordained). On "through faith in his blood," the witnesses note faith the instrument, the blood (Christ's atoning death) the ground.

On "to declare his righteousness for the remission of sins that are past, through the forbearance of God," the witnesses note the purpose. Robertson and Calvin note "remission/passing-over" (paresin, distinct from aphesis) — the passing-over of past sins (committed before Christ, under God's forbearance, not yet fully punished); the atonement vindicating God's righteousness, showing he was just in forbearing. The verse displays Christ the propitiation, declaring God's righteousness in passing over past sins.

The witnesses agree God set forth Christ as the propitiation through faith in his blood, to declare his righteousness for the passing-over of past sins through his forbearance — but divide on hilasterion. Barnes, Clarke, and Theodoret take it as the mercy-seat (Christ the true mercy-seat, where God is reconciled); Robertson and Calvin lean to "propitiatory sacrifice/gift" (that which renders God propitious). "Set forth" is "exhibited publicly" (Barnes, Robertson) or "purposed beforehand" (Calvin allows); "through faith in his blood" makes faith the instrument, Christ's atoning death the ground. The "passing-over" (paresin, distinct from remission) is of past sins committed under God's forbearance, the atonement vindicating his righteousness. The verse displays Christ the propitiation declaring God's righteousness. The verse marks the propitiation: mercy-seat or sacrifice. The verse displays the public exhibition. The verse displays faith in his blood. The verse displays the passing-over of past sins. Christ the propitiation displays the heart of the atonement and the vindication of God's righteousness. The verse displays the reconciliation: God made propitious. The verse displays the ground: Christ's blood. The verse displays the forbearance: past sins passed over. Christ set forth as the propitiation through faith in his blood, declaring God's righteousness for the passing-over of past sins, displays the heart of the atonement: Christ the means by which God is reconciled, his blood the ground of justification, and his death the vindication of God's righteousness in having forborne to punish the sins of former ages. The verse displays the great mystery of the cross — God just in pardoning — and sets up its full statement (v.26): just and the justifier. (This was the verse that converted the poet Cowper from despair.)
26:
"To declare, I say, at this time his righteousness: that he might be just, and the justifier of him which believeth in Jesus." The witnesses note the central crux: God just and the justifier. On "at this time his righteousness," the grammatical voices note the gospel age. Robertson and Calvin note the repetition (from v.25) is emphatic — now, in the gospel age, God's righteousness is fully declared, in contrast with the former times of forbearance.

On "that he might be just, and the justifier," the witnesses note the heart of the gospel. Barnes, Robertson, and JFB note this is the key: God remains just (maintaining the integrity of his law and his character, not pardoning without atonement) while justifying the believer. Robertson: "Nowhere has Paul put the problem of God more acutely." To pardon without atonement would sacrifice justice; the cross alone reconciles God's justice and mercy.

On "him which believeth in Jesus," the witnesses note the condition. The one justified is he who believes in Jesus — faith the instrument by which the believer receives the benefit. The verse displays the central crux: God just and the justifier of the believer.

The witnesses agree this is the heart of the gospel: God declares his righteousness now (the gospel age, in contrast with the former forbearance, Robertson, Calvin) that he might be just and the justifier of him who believes in Jesus. Barnes, Robertson, and JFB note the crux: God remains just (maintaining the integrity of his law and character, not pardoning without atonement) while justifying the believer — the cross alone reconciling justice and mercy ("nowhere has Paul put the problem of God more acutely," Robertson). The condition is faith in Jesus. The verse displays the central crux: God just and the justifier. The verse marks the gospel age: righteousness now declared. The verse displays the crux: just and justifier. The verse displays the reconciliation: justice and mercy met. The verse displays the condition: faith in Jesus. The central crux displays the grand mystery and glory of the gospel. The verse displays the justice: God's law upheld. The verse displays the justification: the believer pardoned. The verse displays the cross: the meeting-place. That God might be just and the justifier of him who believes in Jesus displays the central crux and crowning glory of the gospel: God, in the atonement of Christ, remains perfectly just — upholding the integrity of his law, not pardoning without satisfaction — while at the same time justifying the believing sinner. The verse displays the cross as the place where God's justice and mercy meet, the solution to the deepest problem of how a holy God can pardon the guilty. The verse closes the great propitiation passage and sets up the exclusion of boasting (v.27).
27:
"Where is boasting then? It is excluded. By what law? of works? Nay: but by the law of faith." The witnesses note the exclusion of boasting by the law of faith. On "where is boasting? It is excluded," the grammatical voices note the result. Barnes, Robertson, and Theodoret note that since justification is by grace through faith, all ground of boasting (the Jews' self-glorying in works and privilege) is shut out, completely excluded (Robertson: "shut out").

On "by what law? of works? Nay: but by the law of faith," the witnesses note the principle. Barnes and Ellicott note "law" here means principle or rule; boasting is not excluded by a law of works (which would leave room for self-glory if one could keep it) but by the law (principle) of faith — which receives all from God and brings nothing of its own.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note: justification by faith forever shuts out boasting, for faith ascribes all to God's mercy and nothing to man. The verse displays the exclusion of boasting by the law of faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: since justification is by grace through faith, all boasting (the Jews' self-glorying in works and privilege) is excluded, completely shut out (Robertson, Barnes, Theodoret) — not by a law of works (which would leave room for self-glory) but by the law (principle) of faith, which receives all from God and brings nothing of its own (Barnes, Ellicott). Henry and Calvin note: justification by faith forever shuts out boasting, ascribing all to God's mercy. The verse displays the exclusion of boasting. The verse marks the result: boasting excluded. The verse displays the principle: the law of faith. The verse displays the contrast: works vs faith. The verse displays the ground: faith brings nothing of its own. The exclusion of boasting displays the humbling nature of justification by faith. The verse displays the shutting-out: no room for self-glory. The verse displays the principle: faith, not works. The verse displays the ascription: all to God. The exclusion of boasting by the law of faith displays the humbling and God-glorifying nature of justification by faith: since the sinner is justified freely by grace, receiving all from God and contributing nothing, all ground of self-glory is forever shut out. The verse displays the great truth that the gospel leaves no room for human boasting, ascribing the whole of salvation to God's mercy. The verse sets up the conclusion: justified by faith without works (v.28).
28:
"Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law." The witnesses note the great conclusion: justified by faith without works of law. On "we conclude," the grammatical voices note the summation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the conclusion (logizometha, we reckon/hold) of his whole argument: a man is justified by faith, apart from works of law.

On "without the deeds of the law," the witnesses note the exclusion of works. Barnes notes this is the grand peculiarity of Christianity, the article on which the Reformation turned — Luther called it "the article on which the church stands or falls." Works are not the ground of justification, though believers do good works as the fruit.

On the reconciliation with James, the Reformed Calvin notes James (faith without works is dead) treats a different question — not how we are justified before God, but how faith is proved genuine; James uses "justify" of proving, Paul of accounting righteous. The verse displays the great conclusion: justified by faith without works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul draws the great conclusion (logizometha, we hold, Robertson) — a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law; the grand peculiarity of Christianity, Luther's "article on which the church stands or falls" (Barnes), works not the ground of justification though the fruit of it. Calvin reconciles James: James treats how faith is proved genuine (using "justify" of proving), not how we are justified before God (Paul's sense). The verse displays the great conclusion. The verse marks the summation: justified by faith. The verse displays the exclusion: without works of law. The verse displays the cardinal article: the church's standing or falling. The verse displays the harmony with James: different questions. The great conclusion displays the cardinal doctrine of justification by faith. The verse displays the summary: faith alone justifies. The verse displays the exclusion: not by works. The verse displays the Reformation watchword. The great conclusion — a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law — displays the cardinal doctrine of the gospel and the Reformation, the article on which the church stands or falls: that justification before God is by faith alone, apart from works of law, though genuine faith ever produces good works as its fruit. The verse displays the heart of Paul's argument and the foundation of evangelical faith. The verse sets up the universal application: God of Gentiles too (vv.29-30).
29:
"Is he the God of the Jews only? is he not also of the Gentiles? Yes, of the Gentiles also:" The witnesses note the universality of God and his salvation. On "the God of the Jews only?" the grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note the Jews supposed God belonged to them alone; Paul asks whether God is not also the God of the Gentiles — and answers, "Yes, of the Gentiles also."

On the argument, the witnesses note the inference from justification by faith. Since justification is by faith (not by law or Jewish privilege), it is open to all; therefore God is the God of all, Jew and Gentile alike.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: God designs to manifest himself as Savior to the Gentiles too, extending righteousness and salvation to all nations — opening the kingdom of Christ to the whole world. The verse displays the universality of God and his salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Jews supposed God belonged to them alone, but Paul asks whether God is not also the God of the Gentiles, answering "Yes, of the Gentiles also" (Barnes, Robertson) — the inference from justification by faith: since it is by faith (not law or privilege), it is open to all, so God is the God of all. Calvin notes God designs to manifest himself as Savior to the Gentiles too, extending salvation to all nations. The verse displays the universality of God and his salvation. The verse marks the question: God of the Jews only? The verse displays the answer: of the Gentiles also. The verse displays the inference: justification by faith open to all. The verse displays the scope: all nations. The universality of God displays the breadth of his salvation. The verse displays the correction: God not Israel's alone. The verse displays the reach: to the Gentiles. The verse displays the ground: faith open to all. The universality of God and his salvation — God of the Gentiles as well as the Jews — displays the breadth of the gospel: since justification is by faith and not by Jewish privilege, God is the God and Savior of all nations, opening the kingdom of Christ to the whole world. The verse displays the great truth that flows from justification by faith: the inclusion of the Gentiles on equal terms with the Jews. The verse sets up the oneness of God and the one way of justification (v.30).
30:
"Seeing it is one God, which shall justify the circumcision by faith, and uncircumcision through faith." The witnesses note the oneness of God and the one way of justification. On "one God," the grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the unity of God grounds the unity of salvation: since there is one God, he justifies both Jew (circumcision) and Gentile (uncircumcision) the same way, by faith.

On "by faith... through faith," the witnesses note the slight variation. Robertson, Clarke, and Ellicott note "by faith" (ek pisteos) and "through faith" (dia tes pisteos) mean essentially the same — faith as source and faith as means; the variation is rhetorical, perhaps gently mocking the Jewish notion of a difference, for there is none.

The Reformed Calvin notes the irony: if any wishes a difference between Jew and Gentile, let him take this — the one justified "by faith," the other "through faith"; but it is the same faith, the same way. The verse displays the oneness of God and the one way of justification for all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the unity of God grounds the unity of salvation — since there is one God, he justifies both Jew (circumcision) and Gentile (uncircumcision) the same way, by faith (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); "by faith" and "through faith" mean essentially the same (faith as source and means, the variation rhetorical, Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott). Calvin notes the irony: if any wishes a difference, let him take this — but it is the same faith, the same way. The verse displays the oneness of God and the one way of justification. The verse marks the ground: one God. The verse displays the unity: both justified by faith. The verse displays the variation: by faith, through faith. The verse displays the sameness: one way for all. The oneness of God displays the oneness of the way of salvation. The verse displays the inference: one God, one justification. The verse displays the equality: Jew and Gentile alike. The verse displays the irony: no real difference. The oneness of God and the one way of justification for all — both Jew and Gentile justified by faith — displays the unity of salvation grounded in the unity of God: since there is one God, there is one way of justification, by faith, for all peoples alike. The verse displays the great truth that the unity of God excludes any distinction in the way of salvation, leveling Jew and Gentile before the one God who justifies all who believe. The verse sets up the final objection: does faith make void the law? (v.31).
31:
"Do we then make void the law through faith? God forbid: yea, we establish the law." The witnesses note the final objection and answer: faith establishes the law. On "do we make void the law?" the grammatical voices note the objection. Barnes and Ellicott note the natural objection: does justification by faith abolish or nullify the law (the antinomian charge that faith makes the law of no effect)?

On "God forbid," the witnesses note the emphatic denial. Paul vehemently rejects the idea that faith makes void the law.

On "we establish the law," the witnesses note the positive answer. Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin note faith establishes (confirms, upholds) the law in several ways: God showed his regard for the law in requiring atonement (not pardoning without it); Christ magnified and fulfilled the law; and the justified believer, by faith working through love, is led to obey it. Clarke warns against antinomianism: faith works by love, and love is the principle of obedience; the believer receives power to live in holy obedience. The verse displays the final answer: faith establishes, not voids, the law.

The witnesses agree: to the objection whether justification by faith makes void the law (the antinomian charge, Barnes, Ellicott), Paul vehemently denies ("God forbid") and answers that faith establishes the law — confirming and upholding it: God showed his regard for the law in requiring atonement (not pardoning without it), Christ magnified and fulfilled it, and the justified believer, by faith working through love, is led to obey it (Barnes, Clarke, Calvin). Clarke warns against antinomianism: faith works by love, the principle of obedience. The verse displays the final answer: faith establishes the law. The verse marks the objection: does faith void the law? The verse displays the denial: God forbid. The verse displays the answer: we establish the law. The verse displays the ways: atonement, Christ's fulfillment, the believer's obedience. The final answer displays the harmony of faith and the law. The verse displays the rejection: faith does not abolish law. The verse displays the establishment: faith upholds the law. The verse displays the guard against antinomianism. That faith establishes, not voids, the law displays the harmony of justification by faith with the moral law: far from abolishing the law, the gospel of grace upholds and confirms it — God honoring the law in the atonement, Christ fulfilling it, and the believer, justified by faith, led by love to obey it. The verse displays the apostle's guard against antinomianism and the deep consistency of grace and obedience, closing the great argument of justification by faith. The verse displays that the gospel does not destroy but fulfills the righteous demands of God's law.
